coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots: Effective stabilizing agents for enhanced graphene oxide colloidal dispersions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

Roghani-Mamaqani, Hossein
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), with their unique structure, may have exceptional potential in conductive inks for printed electronics due to their biocompatibility, high aqueous solubility, tunable fluorescence, and noticeable electrical properties. In this study, GQDs were synthesized from citric acid, and nitrogen- doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) was achieved to further enhance their functionality. Comprehensive characterization using FTIR, UV-Vis, photoluminescence, FE-SEM, DLS, and XRD demonstrated their optical, morphological, and structural properties, as well as dispersion stability. Incorporating GQDs into graphene oxide (GO) improved the electrical conductivity and dispersion stability of the composites, with a 2:1 GQD-to-GO ratio identified as optimal for reducing the coffee ring effect in printed applications. Nitrogen doping further enhanced conductivity and uniformity, making the inks more efficient and stable. This study underscores the critical role of GQDs and N-GQDs in developing advanced graphene-based conductive inks, paving the way for next-generation printed electronic devices, including flexible electronics, biosensors, and wearables.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene Quantum dots
Nitrogen Doping
Conductive Inks
Graphene Oxide
Dispersion Stability
Coffee Ring

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
Preparation of dispersed samples of GO and GQD, N-GQD
Analysis of synthesized Graphene oxide, GQD and N-GQD
Dispersion stability analysis of GO and graphene quantum dot composites
Investigation of the coffee ring effect using graphene quantum dots and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of graphene oxide nanosheets: (a) UV-Vis absorption spectrum showing key absorption features, (b) Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrum identifying functional groups, (c) X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern indicating crystallinity and structural order, and (d) Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) images revealing surface morphology and nanosheet structure.

Comprehensive characterization of graphene quantum dots (GQDs): (a) UV-Vis absorption spectrum illustrating the primary absorption features, (b) Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrum identifying specific functional groups, (c) X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern indicating crystallinity and structural order, (d) Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) images showing particle morphology, (e) size distribution by volume of synthesized GQDs, highlighting uniformity, and (f) photoluminescence spectra at various excitation wavelengths, revealing wavelength-dependent emission properties.

Characterization of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs): (a) UV-Vis absorption spectrum highlighting key absorption features, (b) Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrum showing functional groups and confirming nitrogen doping, (c) X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern revealing crystallinity and structural characteristics, (d) Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) images displaying particle morphology, (e) size distribution by volume of synthesized N-GQDs, indicating uniform particle size, and (f) photoluminescence spectra at different excitation wavelengths, illustrating enhanced wavelength-dependent emission due to nitrogen incorporation.

Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) analysis of elemental composition for (a) graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and (b) nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), illustrating differences in elemental distribution with nitrogen incorporation.
(a) Effect of increasing GQD concentration on the zeta potential of graphene oxide dispersion. (b) Impact of nitrogen-doped GQD (N-GQD) on the zeta potential of the dispersion.

UV–visible spectra showing the dispersion stability of graphene oxide under accelerated testing conditions. (a) Graphene oxide without graphene quantum dots (GQD) shows increased sedimentation and decreased absorption intensity with longer centrifugation times. (b-f) The addition of increasing amounts of GQD significantly enhances dispersion stability, reducing sedimentation and maintaining higher absorption intensity due to improved electrostatic repulsion and steric hindrance.

UV–visible spectra illustrating the stability of graphene oxide dispersions under accelerated conditions. (a) In the absence of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQD), graphene oxide shows greater sedimentation and a decline in absorption intensity with longer centrifugation times. (b-f) As the concentration of N-GQD increases, dispersion stability improves, with less sedimentation and higher absorption intensity, attributed to enhanced electrostatic and steric effects.
Bar graphs representing the dispersion stability of the samples under different conditions, based on the area under the UV–visible spectrum of the samples.
Plot showing the difference in the area under the curve for each sample over different time intervals, used to assess dispersion stability.

Optical images showing the coffee ring effect of droplets (100 µL) on glass slides after complete solvent evaporation. (a) Pure GO, (b) GO.100 % GQD, (c) GO.200 % GQD, (d) GO.100 % N-GQD, (e) GO.200 % N-GQD. The W/R ratio, which compares the thickness of the peripheral ring (W) to the diameter of the dried droplet (R), is used to evaluate the coffee ring effect.

Optical images of droplet drying patterns on preheated glass slides (60°C), illustrating the effect of thermal Marangoni flows on suppressing the coffee ring effect. (a) Pure GO, (b) GO.100 % GQD, (c) GO.200 % GQD, (d) GO.100 % N-GQD, (e) GO.200 % N-GQD.

Impedance analysis of optimized samples using Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) over a frequency range of 10–100,000 Hz, before and after thermal reduction at 200°C for one hour. (a) Pure graphene oxide, (b) Pure Graphene quantum dots, (c) Pure Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots, (d) The graphene oxide sample with 200 % by weight graphene quantum dots, (e) Graphene oxide with 200 % nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots.
The prepared formulation for different samples in this research.

Summary of elemental composition for graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) as measured by EDS, highlighting the presence of nitrogen in N-GQDs and comparative elemental ratios.
W/R ratios of graphene oxide dispersions with varying concentrations of graphene quantum dots (GQD) and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQD).
Quantitative data showing the W/R ratios of graphene oxide (GO) dispersions with GQD and N-GQD under thermal Marangoni conditions.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots: Effective stabilizing agents for enhanced graphene oxide colloidal dispersions

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), with their unique structure, may have exceptional potential in conductive inks for printed electronics due to their biocompatibility, high aqueous solubility, tunable fluorescence, and noticeable electrical properties. In this study, GQDs were synthesized from citric acid, and nitrogen- doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) was achieved to further enhance their functionality. Comprehensive characterization using FTIR, UV-Vis, photoluminescence, FE-SEM, DLS, and XRD demonstrated their optical, morphological, and structural properties, as well as dispersion stability. Incorporating GQDs into graphene oxide (GO) improved the electrical conductivity and dispersion stability of the composites, with a 2:1 GQD-to-GO ratio identified as optimal for reducing the coffee ring effect in printed applications. Nitrogen doping further enhanced conductivity and uniformity, making the inks more efficient and stable. This study underscores the critical role of GQDs and N-GQDs in developing advanced graphene-based conductive inks, paving the way for next-generation printed electronic devices, including flexible electronics, biosensors, and wearables.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Conductive inks have a wide range of applications, including printed electronics, flexible displays, RFID tags, and sensors [1]. As technology progresses, the ongoing development and refinement of these inks continue to expand their potential, driving innovation across various industries. Conductive ink allows printed objects to carry electrical current. These inks typically contain conductive materials such as silver, graphite, carbon nanotubes, or carbon particles, which give them their conductive properties [2–5].

On another hand, in graphene’s structure, each carbon atom forms conjugated bonds with neighboring carbon atoms [6]. This arrangement creates a highly conductive material because the fourth valence electron of each carbon atom remains delocalized, contributing to a shared electron cloud that facilitates electrical conductivity [7]. Graphene’s unique properties make it ideal for conductive inks: it is incredibly thin yet remarkably strong, and its large surface area significantly enhances electrical and thermal conductivity [8]. These characteristics support the development of flexible, lightweight, and durable electronic devices. Graphene oxide (GO), another carbon-based material used in conductive inks, has oxygen-containing groups attached to its carbon lattice [9,10]. These functional groups improve the dispersibility of GO in water and other solvents, making it easier to process and print [11,12]. Although GO is less conductive than pure graphene, it can be chemically or thermally reduced to partially restore its conductivity, offering a balance between processability and performance [13].

The integration of nanoparticles with graphene oxide (GO) has been widely investigated to enhance the electrical conductivity of conductive inks, leading to significant advancements in printed electronics. Graphene oxide itself possesses unique electrical properties; however, its conductivity is limited compared to reduced graphene oxide or pure graphene. Researchers have explored various nanoparticles, including silver, copper, and carbon-based materials, to improve the overall performance of GO-based conductive inks [14,15].

Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) are among the most studied additives due to their excellent conductivity. Liu et al. [16] demonstrated that incorporating AgNPs into GO-based inks resulted in a substantial increase in electrical conductivity, attributed to the formation of conductive networks within the ink matrix. This synergistic effect not only enhances conductivity but also improves the mechanical properties of the composite material, making it suitable for flexible electronic applications. Copper nanoparticles (CuNPs) have also been employed to enhance the conductivity of GO inks. A study by Fu et al. [17] reported that CuNPs effectively increased the conductivity of GO composites due to their high intrinsic conductivity. Additionally, the study highlighted the importance of optimizing the weight ratio of CuNPs to GO to achieve maximum conductivity while maintaining the stability of the ink. Moreover, carbon-based nanoparticles, such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and carbon black, have been utilized to enhance the conductivity of GO-based inks [18]. Research by yuan et al. [19] revealed that the incorporation of CNTs into GO matrices significantly improved electrical conductivity due to their exceptional electrical properties and ability to form interconnected networks.

In recent years, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have emerged as a breakthrough in nanomaterials research, especially when combined with graphene nanosheets [20]. This combination leverages the exceptional conductivity and mechanical strength of graphene, while GQDs contribute enhanced photoluminescence and quantum confinement effects. GQDs, which are typically smaller than 20 nm, offer several advantages. Their small size enables precise control over their electronic and optical properties, due to the quantum confinement phenomenon, making them highly versatile for advanced applications in nanotechnology and electronics [21].

The quantum confinement effect in GQDs results in unique behaviors, such as tunable band gaps and enhanced interactions with light, making them valuable for applications in sensor technology and optoelectronics. Additionally, GQDs exhibit low toxicity and chemical stability, making them suitable for biomedical uses such as bioimaging and drug delivery [22]. Their biocompatibility further broadens their potential in critical medical applications. In energy-related fields, graphene quantum dots show promise in improving the efficiency of solar cells and catalytic processes, thanks to their high surface area and abundant reactive sites. Furthermore, their stability and excellent conductivity make them ideal candidates for energy storage devices, including supercapacitors and batteries [23].

Additionally, recent advancements in understanding the coffee ring effect have led to significant findings. Research by Zhang [24] demonstrated that incorporating water-soluble polymer materials can induce the Marangoni effect, effectively suppressing the coffee ring phenomenon. Meanwhile, Bansal and his colleagues [25] have shown that increasing the droplet diameter on hydrophobic surfaces can effectively mitigate the coffee ring effect.

The integration of graphene nanosheets with graphene quantum dots represents a synergistic approach to leveraging advanced materials for a wide range of applications, from biomedical diagnostics to energy conversion and storage. Ongoing research continues to explore and unlock the full potential of these nanomaterials, driving innovation across multiple disciplines and paving the way for next-generation technologies.

In this study, our primary objective was to investigate the formulation of stable suspensions using different weight ratios of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in graphene oxide (GO). We conducted experiments under varied and controlled conditions to examine how these ratios influence the stability of the suspensions. Following the preparation phase, we analyzed the results to identify the most stable suspensions. These samples were then subjected to detailed studies focusing on their electrical conductivity and the coffee ring effect, which is a critical phenomenon in the deposition of colloidal particles. By examining these properties, we aimed to gain deeper insights into how the ratio of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to graphene oxide (GO) affects both the structural integrity and functional performance of the composite material. The outcomes of our investigations provide valuable data to guide future efforts in optimizing the integration of GQDs with GO for various applications. Understanding the dynamics of stability and conductivity is essential for advancing the development of advanced materials, particularly in fields such as electronics, photonics, and energy storage.

Graphite powder, the raw material for synthesizing graphene and graphene oxide, was sourced from Asbury (USA). This high-quality graphite served as the foundation for further chemical modifications. Sulfuric acid, citric acid, potassium permanganate, hydrogen peroxide, sodium hydroxide, and urea, obtained from Merck (Germany), were used as reagents in the oxidation and reduction processes necessary for the synthesis of graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots. Sulfuric acid played a crucial role in the oxidation of graphite, while potassium permanganate was a powerful oxidizing agent that facilitated the conversion of graphite into graphene oxide. Citric acid served as a carbon source for the synthesis of graphene quantum dots, and hydrogen peroxide was employed for its strong oxidizing properties, aiding in the purification process.

Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized using a modified method mentioned in our previous research [26]. Briefly, at initial step, 20 ml of concentrated sulfuric acid was carefully mixed with 2 g of potassium permanganate in an ice bath, and the mixture was stirred for 30 minutes to ensure thorough dispersion of the oxidizing agent. Subsequently, 0.5 g of expanded graphite powder was gradually added to the mixture, followed by continuous stirring for 2 hours. This step facilitated the oxidation of the graphite, leading to the incorporation of oxygen-containing functional groups.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized following a previously reported procedure [1]. In brief, 2 g of citric acid were placed in an oven set to 200°C and heated for 30 minutes. During this heating process, the citric acid underwent liquefaction, with its color gradually changing from colorless to a deep orange, indicating the formation of carbonaceous materials. The citric acid was in the liquid state when removed from the oven and allowed to cool slightly. Afterward, it was added dropwise to a solution containing 0.75 g of sodium hydroxide (NaOH) dissolved in 100 ml of distilled water. This step is critical, as the NaOH not only neutralizes the citric acid but also facilitates the formation of GQDs through a process of carbonization. The dropwise addition of melted citric acid was carefully controlled to avoid any sudden reactions. Following the addition, the mixture was stirred vigorously to ensure homogenous mixing and promote the formation of GQDs. After thorough mixing, the solution was left to stand for a specified time to allow for complete reaction and precipitation of the quantum dots. The resulting GQD solution was stored in a refrigerator for later use. Proper storage is essential to maintain the stability and integrity of the GQDs for subsequent applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were synthesized using a simple and effective method. Initially, 4.2 g of citric acid and 3.6 g of urea were dissolved in 100 ml of distilled water to form a uniform and transparent dispersion. This combination of citric acid, a carbon source, and urea, which acts as a nitrogen dopant, is crucial for the formation of N-GQDs. The homogeneous mixture was then transferred into a 120 ml Teflon-lined autoclave, which is designed to withstand high pressures and temperatures. The autoclave was sealed and heated to 180°C for 8 hours. This hydrothermal treatment promotes the carbonization of citric acid and the incorporation of nitrogen into the carbon matrix, resulting in the formation of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots. After the heating period, the autoclave was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. The reaction yielded a crude green product, which indicated the successful synthesis of N-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of dispersed samples of GO and GQD, N-GQD
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the stability of the graphene oxide (GO) dispersion, six samples each of GO/GQD and GO/N-GQD at different weight ratios were prepared, as detailed in Table 1. The dispersions were conducted by first mixing the synthesized GQDs with distilled water under mild stirring. To further improve the dispersion and ensure uniformity, the mixture was subjected to sonication for 30 minutes using a bath sonicator. The samples were subsequently subjected to various analyses under intensified conditions.

The electrical conductivity of the samples was assessed using PGE18 electrochemical impedance spectrometer manufactured by Sharif Solar Co (Iran). Specifically, the samples were prepared by pressing the powdered quantum dots into thin pellets under controlled pressure. Ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectroscopic tests were performed to confirm the synthesis methods of graphene oxide (GO), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), as well as to evaluate the stability of the graphene oxide dispersion over time. The samples were prepared by diluting the dispersions in distilled water to a suitable concentration. Then the solutions were transferred to a clean quartz cuvette for UV-Vis analysis. Particle size and zeta potential measurements were conducted using a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 from Malvern, providing insights into the dispersion stability and the interaction of the particles within the solution. The structure and functional groups of the samples were analyzed using Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy in the wavenumber range of 500–4000 cm⁻¹ , allowing for the identification of specific chemical bonds and functional groups present in the materials. The samples were prepared by mixing the obtained powders with potassium bromide (KBr). The mixture was then pressed into a thin pellet using a hydraulic press. Photoluminescence properties were investigated using a PL spectrometer, and the samples were analyzed with a Jasco FP-750 spectrophotometer to understand their optical characteristics. To conduct the analysis, the dispersions were transferred into a quartz cuvette for PL analysis and the photoluminescence properties were investigated using a PL spectrometer, with the samples being excited at the appropriate wavelength to measure their emission spectra. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted to study the crystal structures and atomic distances, utilizing an X-ray diffraction spectrometer made by Bruker in Germany. The morphology and surface characteristics were examined using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM), which allowed for a detailed visualization of the particle shapes and sizes. A small drop of the dispersion was placed onto a clean glass slide and allowed to dry under ambient conditions. The dried samples were then coated with a thin layer of gold to enhance conductivity before being analyzed under the FE-SEM. Finally, elemental analysis was performed using X-ray Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) to determine the elemental composition of the samples, contributing to the comprehensive characterization of the synthesized materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of synthesized Graphene oxide, GQD and N-GQD
ce:section.ce:para: Different analysis were conducted on the synthesized graphene oxide (GO), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) to explore their structural and optical characteristics.

The UV-Vis absorption spectrum for GO, revealed a prominent absorption peak at 338 nm ( Fig. 1-a). Following this, the absorption spectrum of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was examined, revealing a peak at 360 nm attributed to a π-π* transition ( Fig. 2-a). This transition reflects the electronic structure of GQDs and underscores their size-dependent optical properties [27].This peak is associated with the electronic transitions in the GQDs and reflects their size-dependent properties, where smaller particles tend to exhibit distinct optical behaviors due to quantum confinement effects [28]. Interestingly, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) displayed an absorption peak at 340 nm, also corresponding to a π-π* transition ( Fig. 3-a). These results indicate that GQDs exhibit higher absorption than GO, while N-GQDs demonstrate even stronger absorption compared to both GQDs and GO [29].

The Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrum of GO as shown in Fig. 1-b indicated a prominent hydroxyl (–OH) peak at 3434 cm⁻¹ and a peak corresponding to C–O stretching vibrations at 1211 cm⁻¹ , confirming the successful oxidation of the graphite precursor [30]. Additionally, C–H bending vibrations at 1397 cm⁻¹ and CC stretching vibrations at 1629 cm⁻¹ suggest partial preservation of the original graphite structure during oxidation. In the case of GQDs, the FTIR spectrum as shown in Fig. 2-b revealed the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups, with an –OH bending vibration peak at 3448 cm⁻¹ and CC stretching bonds at 1637 cm⁻¹ . Notably, a weak peak at 2076 cm⁻¹ suggests incomplete carbonization and the presence of a disordered structure typical for materials at the nanoscale. For N-GQDs as shown in Fig. 3-b, the FTIR spectrum exhibited a broad peak between 3500 and 3000 cm⁻¹ , indicative of both –OH and –NH stretching vibrations. The analysis also identified additional functional groups, including CC stretching vibrations at 1633 cm⁻¹ and amine C–N stretching vibrations at 1324 cm⁻¹ , confirming successful nitrogen incorporation into the GQD structure and enhancing their chemical functionality.

To further understand the structural properties of these materials, X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted. The XRD pattern of GO (Fig. 1-c) displayed a characteristic and intense peak at 2θ = 10.24°, indicating an interlamellar distance of approximately 0.8 nm, as determined by the Bragg’s equation. This value confirms the successful oxidation of graphite to form GO, as the increased interlayer spacing reflects the introduction of oxygen-containing functional groups. In contrast, the XRD patterns of GQDs (Fig. 2-c) revealed a weak broad peak at 2θ = 25°, suggesting an interplanar spacing of 0.34 nm. This broadening indicates the presence of small and disordered carbon structures within the GQDs, reflecting their nanoscale dimensions and the quantum confinement effects. Similarly, the XRD pattern for N-GQDs (Fig. 3-c) features a peak at 2θ = 23°, corresponding to an interplanar distance of 0.37 nm, exhibiting a more defined structure than GQDs. This sharper peak implies enhanced crystallinity due to nitrogen doping, potentially stabilizing the structure and improving the electronic properties.

Subsequently, the absorption results obtained from UV–visible spectroscopy analysis identified the maximum absorption for GQDs at 360 nm. Photoluminescence tests conducted with excitation wavelengths set 20 nm above and below this maximum absorption wavelength revealed that excitation at 340 nm and 350 nm resulted in lower emission intensities compared to the maximum emission observed at 360 nm as shown in Fig. 2-f. Conversely, excitation at 370 nm and 380 nm produced emission intensities that were still lower than that at 360 nm but greater than those observed at 340 nm and 350 nm. Under ultraviolet radiation, GQDs exhibited distinct blue-green fluorescence, highlighting their photoluminescent properties.

In addition, the photoluminescence test results at Fig. 3-f for N-GQDs demonstrated a maximum absorption at 340 nm. Similar to GQDs, excitation for the photoluminescence tests was performed 20 nm above and below this maximum absorption wavelength. The results revealed that excitation wavelengths of 330 nm and 320 nm produced lower emissions compared to the maximum emission at 340 nm. Excitation at 350 nm and 360 nm yielded emissions that were lower than at 340 nm but higher than those at 330 nm and 320 nm. Notably, the emission intensity of N-GQDs was significantly enhanced under ultraviolet radiation, exhibiting a brighter blue-green fluorescence compared to GQDs. These results suggest that nitrogen doping effectively enhances the photoluminescent properties of graphene quantum dots.

Dynamic light scattering (DLS) tests were conducted to further investigate the particle size distribution of the synthesized materials. As illustrated in Fig. 2-e, the particle size of GQDs was found to be less than 10 nm, with an average size of 2 nm. In contrast, as shown in Fig. 3-e the particle size of N-GQDs was significantly larger, measuring approximately 80 nm, which can be attributed to the functionalization of GQDs with nitrogen.

Lastly, the field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) images shown in Fig. 1-d illustrated the morphology of GO confirming the characteristic planar structure of synthesized graphene oxide. The FE-SEM images of GQDs shown in Fig. 2-d reveal a spherical morphology. Furthermore, the FE-SEM image of N-GQDs illustrated in Fig. 3-d reveals larger, hemispherical colloidal particles compared to GQDs. This suggests that the incorporation of nitrogen during the doping process leads to structural changes, resulting in larger particles.

The elemental analysis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) was conducted using Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS), as presented in Fig. 4 and summarized in Table 2. The EDS results for GQDs indicate the presence of carbon, oxygen, and sodium, confirming the successful synthesis of GQDs utilizing citric acid as the carbon source and sodium hydroxide as a reagent. In addition, the EDS analysis of N-GQDs demonstrates the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, validating the effective doping of GQDs with nitrogen. Overall, the elemental analysis confirms that the synthesis methods employed resulted in the successful fabrication of both GQDs and N-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dispersion stability analysis of GO and graphene quantum dot composites
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, we investigated the stability properties of graphene oxide dispersion by conducting zeta potential analysis on pre-prepared samples as detailed in Table 1. The zeta potential is a critical parameter that reflects the balance of attractive and repulsive forces between particles in a dispersion; specifically, higher absolute values indicate increased electrostatic stability and a reduced tendency for particle aggregation. Fig. 5-a illustrates that increasing the concentration of graphene quantum dots (GQD) within the graphene oxide dispersion results in a more negative zeta potential. The negative charge contributed by GQD enhances electrostatic repulsion among the graphene oxide sheets, promoting dispersion stability. This phenomenon can be attributed to the increased surface area and functional groups on GQD, which enhance charge distribution. Consequently, higher amounts of GQD lead to greater electrostatic repulsion, minimizing the likelihood of cluster formation in the dispersion. In contrast, Fig. 5-b demonstrates the impact of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQD) on the zeta potential of the graphene oxide dispersion. The introduction of positively charged nitrogen atoms into the N-GQD structure reduces the overall negative charge of the dispersion. This alteration leads to diminished electrostatic repulsion among particles, primarily because nitrogen atoms can partially neutralize the negative surface charges present on graphene oxide. As a result, there is a decrease in electrostatic repulsion, while still maintaining adequate repulsive forces to prevent excessive aggregation.

In the next step of our research, we investigated the dispersion stability of the samples under accelerated testing conditions. The samples, as outlined in Table 1, were subjected to various intensified conditions, including centrifugation, temperature variation, and freeze-thaw cycles, to evaluate their stability. For the centrifugation method, the samples were centrifuged at 2000 rpm for durations of 10, 15, and 20 minutes, after which each sample underwent UV–visible analysis to assess changes in dispersion stability.

As illustrated in Fig. 6-a, the ultraviolet-visible spectrum of graphene oxide without the addition of graphene quantum dots demonstrated a marked increase in sedimentation over time. Specifically, the amount of deposition increased as centrifugation time extended from 10 to 15 minutes and from 15 to 20 minutes, resulting in a decrease in the absorption intensity of the spectrum. This observation indicates a tendency for the particles to aggregate and settle out of the dispersion in the absence of stabilizing agents.

In contrast, upon the addition of graphene quantum dots in increasing amounts, the stability of graphene oxide was significantly enhanced as shown in Fig. 6-b to Fig. 6-f. The presence of GQD effectively mitigated sedimentation and improved the absorption intensity in the UV–visible spectrum, suggesting better dispersion stability. This enhancement can be attributed to the increased electrostatic repulsion and steric hindrance provided by the GQD, which prevents particle aggregation and maintains a uniform dispersion. These findings reinforce the importance of using graphene quantum dots as stabilizing agents in graphene oxide dispersions, particularly under conditions that would typically promote aggregation.

The same process was repeated for the samples containing nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots, as shown in Fig. 7. The results demonstrate that, similar to the graphene oxide samples with graphene quantum dots, the addition of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQD) in increasing amounts led to improved dispersion stability of the graphene oxide dispersion.

In general, the results indicate that with each increase in centrifugation time, there was a greater amount of sediment formation across all samples, and the absorption intensity in the UV–visible spectrum correspondingly decreased. However, this decrease in intensity was less pronounced in samples containing 100 wt% graphene quantum dots, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots, and 200 wt% graphene quantum dots and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots. This reduced loss of intensity highlights the improved stability of these samples, suggesting that the addition of both types of quantum dots plays a critical role in maintaining stable dispersions, even under intensified conditions such as extended centrifugation times. These findings further corroborate the effectiveness of quantum dots, particularly in higher concentrations, as stabilizing agents in graphene oxide dispersions. The enhanced stability provided by both graphene quantum dots and nitrogen-doped quantum dots suggests a synergistic effect that minimizes particle aggregation and promotes uniform dispersion, even when subjected to accelerated conditions.

According to the ultraviolet-visible spectra, the area under the curve in the 400–700 nm wavelength range was calculated for all samples individually. This approach provided a more accurate quantification of the reduction in absorption over time, which is directly related to the dispersion stability of the samples. The bar graphs in Fig. 8 were constructed using these values, allowing for a clearer comparison of the samples' stability over different conditions.

As shown in part a of the graphs, the dispersion of graphene oxide exhibited greater stability with increasing amounts of graphene quantum dots (GQD), resulting in less precipitation over time. The addition of GQD enhanced the electrostatic repulsion and steric hindrance between graphene oxide particles, reducing their tendency to aggregate and settle out of the dispersion. As a result, the overall absorption in the UV–visible spectrum was maintained for longer periods, indicating improved stability. Part b presents a similar trend, where the stability of graphene oxide also increased with the amount of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQD). The presence of nitrogen-doped quantum dots further reduced the reduction in absorption intensity, as indicated by the smaller differences in the area under the curve. This suggests that N-GQD provides an additional stabilization mechanism, likely due to the nitrogen atoms altering the surface charge distribution of the graphene oxide, which leads to stronger electrostatic and steric stabilization.

A comparison of parts a and b reveals that the graphene oxide sample containing 200 wt% nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots exhibited better stability than the sample containing 200 wt% graphene quantum dots. The small difference in the values of the area under the curve for the nitrogen-doped samples highlights the role of nitrogen doping in enhancing colloidal stability. Nitrogen doping likely improves the surface interactions between the graphene oxide and the surrounding medium, preventing aggregation even more effectively than graphene quantum dots alone. In addition to stabilizing the dispersion, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots also enhance other properties, such as fluorescence intensity, which is valuable for optoelectronic applications. Thus, it can be concluded that nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots not only enhance optical properties but also significantly improve the colloidal stability of graphene oxide dispersions, making them a more versatile and efficient stabilizing agent compared to regular graphene quantum dots.

The differences in the data obtained from the area under the curve for each sample were calculated and plotted over different time intervals. These calculations allowed for a more detailed evaluation of the dispersion stability. Fig. 9 shows that as the amount of graphene quantum dots (GQD) and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQD) in the graphene oxide increases, the difference between the area under the curves decreases. This trend suggests a more stable dispersion with less variation in absorption over time, as the presence of quantum dotsparticularly at higher concentrations—helps prevent particle aggregation. Notably, the graphene oxide samples containing 100 % by weight GQD and N_GQD, as well as those containing 200 % by weight of these materials, showed the smallest differences. This implies that these specific concentrations of GQD and N_GQD are optimal for achieving stable dispersions. The smaller differences between the curves at these concentrations reflect a more uniform and consistent stabilization effect, where the quantum dots effectively counteract the forces that would normally cause the graphene oxide to settle and aggregate.

The results obtained from temperature-accelerated conditions, as well as those intensified by freeze-thaw cycling, closely mirrored the stability outcomes observed in the centrifuge-accelerated method. In all cases, the dispersion stability of graphene oxide samples improved with the increasing addition of graphene quantum dots (GQD) and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQD). These findings suggest that the stabilizing effects of GQD and N-GQD are robust and effective under various stress conditions, not limited to centrifugation alone. The ability of these quantum dots to maintain stability in both elevated temperatures and during freeze-thaw cycles further demonstrates their potential for enhancing the colloidal stability of graphene oxide in diverse environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the coffee ring effect using graphene quantum dots and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the conclusions derived from ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectroscopy, we identified the optimal weight percentages of graphene quantum dots to investigate their influence on the coffee ring effect. Samples containing 100 wt% and 200 wt% of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were found to exhibit the most stable and desirable properties compared to pure graphene oxide, based on their UV-Vis absorption curves. These samples, were photographed and systematically analyzed to assess their impact on the coffee ring effect, a common phenomenon observed during the evaporation of colloidal suspensions.

To compare the coffee ring effect across these samples, droplets with the volume of 100 µL were carefully placed on glass slides at room temperature. Fig. 10 displays the optical images of droplets from both the optimal samples and pure graphene oxide dispersion after complete solvent evaporation. The primary parameter used to quantitatively evaluate the coffee ring effect is the W/R ratio, where W represents the thickness of the peripheral ring formed by particle deposition, and R denotes the diameter of the dried droplet. A W/R value approaching unity (W/R ≈ 1) suggests a reduced or diminished coffee ring effect, indicating more uniform particle deposition across the droplet.

According to the data presented in Table 3, pure graphene oxide exhibited a pronounced coffee ring effect, with a lower W/R value compared to samples containing GQDs and N-GQDs. The introduction of GQDs and N-GQDs appeared to mitigate the coffee ring effect, reducing the accumulation of particles at the droplet's edge. This finding suggests that the incorporation of GQDs and N-GQDs modifies the evaporation dynamics, potentially due to their unique surface chemistry, enhanced wettability, and improved colloidal stability, which collectively lead to more uniform particle distribution during droplet drying.

Building on previous research strategies to suppress the coffee ring effect, we employed the thermal Marangoni effect as an additional method. The thermal Marangoni effect arises due to surface tension gradients induced by temperature differences within the droplet, which can counteract the typical outward flow responsible for particle deposition at the edges. To harness this effect, droplets were deposited on a preheated glass slide maintained at 60°C, in contrast to the earlier room-temperature experiments. The resulting droplet images, shown in Fig. 11, demonstrate the impact of the thermal Marangoni effect after complete evaporation. The quantitative data in Table 4 confirm that applying thermal gradients significantly suppressed the coffee ring effect. In these conditions, particles were driven towards the center of the droplet rather than the periphery, resulting in more uniform drying patterns. This observation is consistent with previous studies, where thermal Marangoni flows induce inward convection, effectively balancing the capillary-driven outward flow responsible for the coffee ring formation. The presence of GQDs and N-GQDs further enhanced the suppression, likely due to their role in modifying surface tension and improving thermal conductivity, which can intensify Marangoni flows. In summary, the addition of graphene quantum dots and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots to graphene oxide dispersion significantly alters the drying dynamics of droplets, reducing the coffee ring effect. When coupled with the thermal Marangoni effect, the suppression of coffee rings is even more pronounced, providing insight into advanced techniques for controlling particle deposition in colloidal systems. These findings have broad implications for improving the uniformity of thin-film coatings, inkjet printing technologies, and other applications reliant on controlled evaporation processes.

In this study, the impedance value of the optimized samples was analyzed using Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) over a frequency range of 10–100,000 Hz. To investigate the effects of thermal treatment, the samples were also subjected to thermal reduction at 200°C for one hour, and impedance measurements were repeated. The results, shown in Fig. 12, reveal distinct behaviors for the different materials tested. For pure graphene oxide as illustrated in Fig. 12-a, the results showed a significant decrease in electrical resistance, dropping by four orders of magnitude when subjected to thermal reduction. This substantial reduction indicates an enhancement in the material's conductivity, suggesting that the thermal treatment effectively restores the graphene sp² structure, thereby improving electron mobility.

In contrast, the impedance analyses of graphene quantum dots in Fig. 12-b demonstrated that their impedance was initially comparable to that of pure graphene oxide. However, unlike the graphene oxide, the graphene quantum dots did not exhibit any noticeable change in resistance after undergoing thermal reduction at 200°C. The thermal reduction process did not significantly alter the conductivity of GQDs due to their already high level of disorder and oxygen-containing functional groups, which could limit the degree of structural transformation upon heating.

Additionally, impedance analyses were conducted for nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots as well as samples containing graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots to understand their electrical properties and the effects of thermal treatment. The results are summarized as follows: In Fig. 12-c, the impedance analysis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots showed that their resistance was similar to both pure graphene oxide and undoped graphene quantum dots. However, like the undoped graphene quantum dots, they did not respond to thermal reduction at 200°C, indicating a stable structure that is unaffected by this thermal process.

As revealed in Fig. 12-d, for the graphene oxide sample containing 200 % by weight of graphene quantum dots, the addition of these quantum dots to graphene oxide had no significant effect on its conductivity, suggesting that the presence of graphene quantum dots alone does not enhance or alter the conductive properties of graphene oxide under these conditions. In Fig. 12-e, the impedance analysis for the graphene oxide sample containing 200 % by weight of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots revealed a different behavior. The addition of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots reduced the sample’s impedance compared to pure graphene oxide, as shown in Fig. 12-a, thereby improving its conductivity. However, after thermal reduction, the resistance of the pure graphene oxide was observed to be lower than that of the graphene oxide containing nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots. This difference is attributed to the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots not responding to the thermal reduction process, which limits further reduction in resistance. Additionally, the improved conductivity of the sample with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots is likely due to the enhanced surface uniformity provided by the doped quantum dots. Further analysis of the coffee ring effect indicated that the incorporation of 200 % by weight of both undoped and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots effectively reduced and eventually eliminated the coffee ring effect, resulting in a smooth and uniform surface. This increased surface smoothness plays a crucial role in enhancing the conductivity properties of the material, as a smoother surface can facilitate better electron transport and reduce scattering, thus contributing to the overall conductivity improvement.

The addition of graphene quantum dots can play a crucial role in hindering the restacking of graphene oxide nanosheets, thereby improving the dispersion state. By preventing restacking, the graphene oxide nanosheets remain in a more sheet-like conformation, which is essential for enhancing electrical conductivity. This improved dispersion and sheet-like structure create pathways that facilitate electron mobility and reduce resistance.

This study highlights significant advancements achieved through the incorporation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) into graphene oxide (GO) for the formulation of stable conductive inks. It was demonstrated that GQDs not only enhance the dispersion stability of GO but also improve its electrical conductivity, particularly at a GQD-to-GO ratio of 2:1, which exhibited superior performance in mitigating the coffee ring effect as a critical consideration in printed electronics. The observed synergy between GQDs and N-GQDs indicates that nitrogen doping further augments these properties, ensuring robust stability even under various stress conditions, including temperature fluctuations and freeze-thaw cycles. The improved dispersion state and structural integrity of the GQD-GO composites contribute to enhanced electron mobility, thereby laying the groundwork for more efficient and reliable conductive inks applicable in advanced electronic devices.

Roghani-Mamaqani Hossein: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation. Mohammadi Mahtab: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Rezvani-Moghaddam Amir: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
